data.title: Blackstone's BREIT: Private Equity Outperformance Is Not What It Appears
data.description:
Blackstone’s (BX) real estate investment vehicle BREIT claims to have an 8.1% return in 2025, which would represent sizable outperformance over public REITs. The average REIT was down in 2025, and even cap-weighted indexes, such as the Vanguard REIT ETF (VNQ), that benefited from mega-cap REIT outperformance, had quite modest returns. VNQ returned 4.17% inclusive of dividends.

Blackstone suggests that its outperformance stems from superior asset selection. They directly compare their performance to that of publicly traded REITs and claim 70% outperformance.

Note this is 70% higher, not 70 percentage points.

While I admire Blackstone’s salesmanship and ability to raise tremendous amounts of AUM, I want to set the record straight on their performance.

Total return is a very different figure for public REITs than it is for private REITs.

Private Versus Public—What Total Return Means

For public markets, there is an established methodology for calculating returns, and it can be verified by the market prices of the stocks. For an individual stock, one can calculate total return by comparing the price at the end of 2025 to the price at the start of 2025 and adding dividends. A portfolio’s performance can be measured similarly by taking the sum product of each position’s return and the weights.

For active portfolios, there is a bit more to it, as one has to factor in trade dates and pricing at the time of each trade, but this has all been standardized. Returns are a factual figure that can be verified.

Private equity has a bit more leeway in the way they report returns, as it is not based on a visible barometer but rather their internal evaluation of NAV. As such, the 2025 total return of BREIT is a comparison of what Blackstone thought those assets were worth at the end of 2024 and what Blackstone thinks they are worth at the end of 2025.

Both figures are a bit fuzzier and harder to verify than they are with public markets.

We think Blackstone’s use of the term outperformance is misleading because it is not an apples-to-apples comparison. They are comparing estimated asset value (BREIT NAV) to public market pricing (publicly traded REITs).

If we dig a bit deeper, I think it becomes clear that this “outperformance” is a meaningless figure. It says quite a bit more about the spread between public REIT prices and NAV of real estate than it does about BREIT’s performance.

Asset Weights and How Each Asset Type Performed

BREIT puts out a nice breakdown of their property sector weights.

We shall now look at the performance of these various asset classes.

As seen on the graph above, Rental housing, industrial, and data centers are the top exposures for BREIT.

Single-family rental was a tough sector in 2025. A wave of supply put pressure on asking rents. We go into greater detail here. As stated in that article, we may look to get into single-family rental now that the pricing on the sector is much cheaper, but it was not a good place to be at the start of 2025. American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) and Invitation Homes (INVH) were each down substantially in 2025.

Multifamily apartments are another big contingent of BREIT’s rental housing. That sector was also tough due to a similar supply wave. Asking rents for apartments nationally were flat to slightly down in 2025. As one would expect in such an environment, the big apartment players were down significantly, with Camden (CPT), Equity Residential (EQR), and Avalon Bay (AVB) down 4%, 10%, and 16%, respectively.

Industrial appears to be the bright spot in BREIT’s portfolio. While industrial had a similar supply wave, there was enough positive mark-to-market of rent that NOI for the sector still increased nicely. Industrial REITs were up about 17% in 2025.

Data centers are a major component of BREIT at 21% of their portfolio. While the sector is hot due to the AI buildout, valuation matters, and at the start of 2025, data centers were trading at sky-high valuations. One could buy data centers as Blackstone did, but to do so, you had to pay a massive premium. Flocking to the hot sector comes with quite a price tag.

Thus, even though data center fundamentals are strong, the sector suffered in 2025. The biggest and best players traded down severely. Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty (DLR) dropped 19% and 13%, respectively, in 2025.

We like strong fundamentals, but we also like value, so we waited until AFTER the price drop in Equinix to jump in.

The other large portions of BREIT are student housing and affordable housing. There is no clear public proxy for student housing as the last student housing REIT, American Campus Communities (formerly ACC), went private in 2022.

The best proxy for affordable housing would be manufactured housing and RVs, with the large-cap REITs in the space being Sun Communities (SUI) and Equity Lifestyle (ELS). Each performed rather poorly in 2025.

BREIT’s “Outperformance” is Really Just a Differing Reporting Methodology

In looking at each of BREIT's main portfolio concentrations, only industrial performed well in 2025. Data centers, apartments, single-family rentals, and affordable housing were all down substantially in 2025.

So how does Blackstone come up with the +8.1% figure?

It comes down to a methodology difference. Real estate fundamentally performed well in 2025. The weakness in publicly traded REITs was related to them getting cheaper relative to the underlying value of their assets.

Publicly traded REITs now trade at a massive discount to net asset value. The median REIT trades at 83% of NAV. Many trade for less than 70% of NAV.

Thus, it is not that BREIT outperformed on a fundamental or NAV basis. Instead, publicly traded REITs simply traded down relative to their NAV.

It strikes me as reasonable that the asset value of BREIT could have risen by 8.1% (inclusive of dividends paid) in 2025. It is not inherently an outrageous figure.

Real estate in general tends to return north of 8% annually. Publicly traded equity REITs averaged compound annual returns of 9.3% over the last 40 years.

In 2025, publicly traded REITs gained somewhere in the ballpark of 8%-9% in fundamental asset value.

The difference is that public REIT returns are reported based on market pricing while BREIT’s returns are reported based on estimated asset value.

Thus, I do not see it as outperformance. Both BREIT and public REITs performed reasonably well fundamentally. The delta in the 2025 return is simply a difference in reporting methodology.

In this article, we compared BREIT’s sectors to the publicly traded performance of the highest caliber REITs.

BREIT employs intelligent people for asset selection. I have no doubt they do a competent job of choosing which properties to buy.

However, in my opinion:

  • It is unrealistic to think their data center selection and management are superior to that of Equinix, which is the preeminent global leader in data centers.
  • It is unrealistic to think their single-family rental platform is better than those of Invitation Homes and American Homes 4 Rent, which boast the highest margins in the business.
  • It is unrealistic to think BREIT can better acquire and manage industrial real estate than behemoths like Prologis (PLD), who literally wrote the white papers that define the sector.

Outperformance of private equity is merely an illusion created by volatile market pricing of publicly traded stocks.

The sales team at Blackstone is correct to spin it as outperformance. That is their job.

It is the job of investors to see through it and invest in what is most opportunistic at any given moment.

BREIT is an entirely reasonable vehicle. Their fees are a bit high, but it is generally an okay place to invest.

However, timing matters, and the worst time to invest in private equity is when it is priced at a huge premium to public equivalents.

Why buy shares of BREIT at 100% of NAV (before fees and above 100% after fees) when you can buy high-quality publicly traded companies at large discounts to NAV? Private equity REITs generally underperform public equity REITs, and the underperformance is greater when the private vehicles are trading at a premium to the public vehicles.

This pattern has played out before.

In December 2022, we had similar gripes with the way BREIT was priced at a vast premium to public REITs.

“Over time, public and private valuations realign, and that means there will be a 30% delta in the other direction. Either private market NAVs are coming down or public market REITs are coming up or some combination of the 2.”

Indeed, BREIT has performed quite poorly since December 2022.

BREIT Performance

Source: Blackstone

We view today as a similar setup.

BREIT is trading at a premium to public companies in the same asset classes and is thus positioned to underperform going forward.

The Actionable Conclusion

Shares of BREIT are redeemable, and importantly, redemption is at the NAV stated by Blackstone.

This allows investors to cash out at what is functionally full NAV and reinvest that capital in public REITs in the same property sectors at much more attractive valuations.

Impact on Blackstone

While I think investors would be wise to consider cashing out of private equity, there is no reason to believe that is actually going to happen at a mass scale.

Blackstone has proven time and time again that they are a capital-raising machine. They are remarkably adept at raising AUM, and that is ultimately the driver of BX. Thus, I am bearish on BREIT and "Neutral" on BX.

data.title: Dorian LPG: Accumulating A Small Holding
data.description:
Shares of very large gas carrier (VLGC) owner-operator Dorian LPG Ltd. (LPG) have rallied 20% since Christmas Eve, spurred by an analyst upgrade citing $70,000 day rates in January 2026. The market is extremely volatile and should remain so as the U.S. is poised to increase its export capacity by 66% from 2024 to 2028 while 108 vessels are scheduled to be delivered 2026 to 2028. With a good balance sheet, an irregular dividend yield of 8.6% over the past 12 months, and potentially bullish Panama Canal dynamics owing to several factors, Dorian merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Dorian LPG, Ltd. is a Marshall Islands domiciled, Stamford, Connecticut headquartered liquified petroleum gas (mainly propane) shipping concern focused on the operation of its 27 very large gas carriers (VLGCs) with an aggregate carrying capacity of ~2.3 million cubic meters (cbm). The company owns 21 of its vessels and is expected to take delivery of a new VLGC in spring 2026. Dorian was founded in 2013 and went public in 2014, raising net proceeds of $127.9 million at $19 per share. Its stock trades around $28.00 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of $1.2 billion.

The company operates on a fiscal year (FY) ending March 31st. For the avoidance of doubt, the 12-month period ending March 31, 2026 is FY26. Unless otherwise noted, day rate pricing references below are in calendar quarters or years.

VLGC Market

The VLGC market in which Dorian participates is relatively small but not terribly concentrated. As of May 23, 2025, its 27 vessels accounted for 7% of a total market consisting of 412 freighters with an aggregate carrying capacity of 33.6 million cbm. The global VLGC fleet is owned by a total of 115 entities worldwide, with the top ten players responsible for 41% of the count. The company lists BW LPG Ltd (BWLP), Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha (NPNYY), and Petredec Pte. Ltd. as its largest competitors.

For the sake of clarity, Dorian does not transport liquefied natural gas, which is primarily methane. The main liquified petroleum gas route [LPG] had traditionally originated in the Arabian Gulf (Ras Tanura, Saudi Arabia) destined for markets in Asia. However, with evolving dynamics related to the Ukrainian conflict and the emergence of the U.S. as the largest export hub – production up 90% since 2015 – Texas to Europe and Asia routes have grown in importance. Unsurprisingly, Dorian’s performance is a function of supply-demand dynamics in the LPG transport industry, which is an affected by a plethora of factors discussed below.

Vessels are generally operated under time charters, bareboat charters, spot charters, or contracts of affreightment (COAs). Time charters involve the charterer (customer) contracting a vessel for a fixed period at set daily or monthly rates, paying voyage expenses such as fuel and port costs. Bareboat charters entail the charterer taking full control of the vessel for a predetermined period, baring full responsibility for the crew and fuel. Spot charters involve the charterer contracting for the transport of LPG from load port to discharge port at an agreed upon freight per ton of cargo carried. In spot market charters, Dorian bears the port and fuel costs. COAs are somewhat of a hybrid, entailing the carriage of multiple cargoes over the same or multiple routes at pre-arranged terms, where Dorain essentially handles all the logistics, whether with its own vessels or others, with voyage and capital costs borne by the shipowner. At the risk of stating the obvious, a portfolio weighted heavily in time charters provides more predictable cash flows vis a vie spot charters, but typically generates lower revenue in rising day rate environments.

Joint Venture & Fleet

In an attempt to better cover its customer base from a geographic perspective back in 2015, Dorian entered into a joint venture with Singapore based MOL Energia (known as the Helios Pool) into which the former has committed its entire fleet and the latter three vessels. The pool can operate in the spot market, pursuant to COAs, or time charters of two years or less. That said, Dorian operates almost exclusively in the spot market with customers ranging from oil giants like Exxon Mobil (XOM) or Chevron (CVX) to commodity traders like Glencore plc (GLCNF) or importers such as Indian Oil.

The average age of the company’s fleet is nine years versus an industry average of ten. Sixteen of the company’s fleet are equipped with sulfur oxide exhaust gas cleaning systems known as scrubbers, which allow them to consume cheaper, high-sulfur-content marine fuel, improving voyage economics. As stated above, it anticipates onboarding a new VLGC in calendar 2Q26, one of 36 expected to be delivered across the industry in calendar 2026.

Share Price Performance

Owing to the volatility of energy complex pricing and finite number of vessels in service, day rates in the VLGC market can oscillate wildly, typically trading lower in the first half of a calendar year and rising in the second half as importers in the Northern Hemisphere build inventories for the winter heating season. Shipping prices are influenced by several factors, including U.S. propane production and consumption, export capacity, the spread between the U.S. (Mont Belvieu) propane market and prices paid in the Far East end markets, and maybe most importantly, sailing patterns – notably dictated by operational capacity at the Panama Canal, through which ~75% of U.S. to Far East LPG shipments transit. With the Permian Basin producing more gas as it ages, the supply for export will not be an issue, especially with U.S. inventories of propane and propylene hitting 23-year highs in late-October 2025. Another variable to factor into the future algebra is the expected addition of 108 vessels with 9.7 million cbm of capacity expected to be delivered from 2026 to YE28, which will be needed to handle the expected 66% jump in U.S. export capacity (2024 to 2028) eventually reaching 113 million tonnes per annum.

Returning to the volatility, on a time-charter equivalent (TCE) basis, day rates traded below $20,000 in June 2020, peaking at an all-time high above $170,000 in September 2023, only to spend most of 2024 between $25,000 and $50,000. 2025 was characterized by uncertainty created by U.S. tariff policy. That said, average day rates rose gradually from ~$31,000 in 1Q25 to ~$55,700 in 4Q25. Reflecting these price swings, Dorian’s stock rallied 750% off of its pandemic-selloff low to $51.66 per share in May 2024. That peak signified a top in market expectations regarding continuing momentum in day rates after the company’s record FY24 (ending March 31, 2024), when it realized an average day rate of $62,129. However, when the VLGC market faded, Dorian’s performance suffered, experiencing a 36% drop in its average day rate during FY25 to $39,778 while its daily operating expenses rose 6% to $11,143, resulting in a 51% downdraft in Adj. EBITDA to $206.0 million. Its stock reflected these realities, bottoming at $16.66 per share shortly after President Trump’s Liberation Day comments spurred uncertainty in international commerce markets during early April 2025.

2QFY26 Financials

Dorian’s stock has since rebounded along with day rates, although its most recent quarterly report of November 6, 2025 was not well-received. The company posted 2QFY26 earnings of $1.31 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $85.7 million on revenue of $123.0 million versus $0.35 per share (non-GAAP) on Adj. EBITDA of $46.2 million on revenue of $81.7 million in 2QFY25, representing somewhat gaudy year-over-year gains of 274%, 85%, and 51% respectively. These improvements were a function of its average day rate improving 45% over the prior year period to $53,725 while daily vessel operating expenses increased 6% to $10,705. However, the market was anticipating day rates in the high 50s and sold shares of LPG 8% lower to $27.17 in the subsequent trading session, eventually bottoming at a six-month low of $23.76 on December 24, 2025.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Despite the market’s disappointment, Dorian increased its ‘irregular’ dividend from $0.60 per share in August 2025 to $0.65 per share in November 2025. Although management will not call it a regular dividend owing to the capriciousness of the VLGC market, it has made four quarterly disbursements every year since achieving that feat in calendar 2022, featuring $1.00 quarterly dividends (save one $2.50 per share exception in May 2022) until falling to $0.70 per share in February 2025. Using its total irregular dividends of $2.45 per share paid in calendar 2025, Dorian’s stock currently yields 8.6%.

As far as its ability to continue to pay its irregular dividend, its balance sheet is in solid stead, reflecting cash and equivalents of $268.3 million versus debt of $526.4 million for net leverage of 1.25, with an additional $150 million of liquidity available through untapped debt financing. The last twelve months of dividends were easily covered by Dorian’s TTM Adj. EBITDA of $206.2 million versus calendar 2025 dividend payments of ~$105.0 million. The company’s board authorized a $100 million share repurchase program in 2022, but it has only bought back $5.6 million of stock since its inception.

After an upgrade from Pareto Securities on January 6, 2026, the Street leans positive on Dorian’s prospects. On average, sell-side analysts expect the company to earn $4.09 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $412.4 million in FY26, followed by $2.69 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $363.2 million in FY27 as more vessels come online in calendar 2026.

CEO, President & Chairman John Hadjipateras is bullish on his company’s outlook, purchasing 15,000 shares at $27.30 on January 7, 2026. The last time he added to his position was in April 2025, wisely using Liberation Day concerns as an opportunity to buy 12,500 shares at $17.75.

Verdict:

Whether his timing is as good as it was before remains to be seen. His stock has already snapped back 20% since Christmas Eve, somewhat bolstered by the Pareto Securities upgrade with analyst Eirik Haavaldsen citing current day rates around $70,000 and what he perceives as modest fleet growth until the back end of calendar 2026 as reasons for his optimism. To be sure, with solid demand out of China, India, and Indonesia and Enterprise Products (EPD) expected to bring its two terminal projects on line in 2026 – one in 1H26 and the other by YE26 – expanding U.S. export capacity by 25%, operating almost exclusively in the spot market should prove fruitful, although the market is subject to bouts of lumpiness with 36 new vessels hitting the market throughout 2026 and Enterprise’s second project still 11 months away.

That said, there are two more dynamics that should work in the VLGC market’s (and by extension, Dorian’s) favor. First, approximately 10% of the global fleet is over 25 years old, which means that although 108 vessels should enter the market over the next three years, ~40 will likely be coming off the market. Second, Panama Canal bottlenecks, which occurred in 2025 are bullish for VLGCs because longer time to move cargo means fewer ships competing for next shipment. There are two factors that should keep the Panama Canal backed up. Notably, it fully implemented a long-term slot allocation system in 2025, auctioning off 40% of its slots in advance, somewhat crowding out non-booked ships. This system will continue (with modifications) in 2026. Also, VLECs, which transport ethane from U.S. to China, are becoming an increasingly large consumer of the canal, averaging eight ships per month in 2025, further congesting the passage. And with continued growth of export capacity out of the U.S. and more vessels coming on the market, more of them will be compelled to take the much longer Cape of Good Hope route to the Far East, potentially putting a floor in the VLGC market.

As such, I have established a small position in LPG with covered call orders. I will look for any pull back under $25 to be used an opportunity to add slightly to the holding, as LPG feels like an Accumulate here.

data.title: Adient: There Are Cheaper Alternatives In 2026
data.description:
I generally like companies that specialize in a segment of production, such as seating. When it comes to things like aerospace interiors, I actually look for companies that do their seating and how to invest in them, both for private jets as well as for other companies. Adient plc (ADNT) is a company I reviewed almost exactly 6 months ago and which has underperformed the market since that article, per my expectations at the time.

In this article, I will review the reasons for this performance (in particular that drop we saw towards the end of the year, followed by a slight surge), as well as what sort of upside can be expected from the company from here on out. It's important to note, however, that Adient's lack of appeal to me is due to a relatively peer-heavy space - yes, even in the seating subsegment. There are companies such as Lear (LEA) that are in the space as well, and while A doesn't mean that B can't get a market share, a crowded space with equal alternatives does make it harder for a company to generate alpha. And true to the site name, "Seeking Alpha" is what I am, after all, about in my writing here.

So, about Adient - the former Johnson Controls (JCI) seating business with a spinoff date now 10 years ago. It's a top-two player (which means take my description above about peers with a pinch of salt), and it's also fair to say that seating is not as interchangeable as some sectors in automotive. It's hard to take on an incumbent manufacturer due to production, distribution, customer trust, and long-term contracts. There's also the simple fact that EVs and autonomous vehicles open the sector up to entirely new dynamics, which could offer new and improved markets for companies like this. It's therefore not illogical at all to find Adient and similar companies attractive.

Just make sure you're buying them at the right price - that, dear reader, is what my goal is to help you with here.

Adient - Recent Results Do Not Spell Out a Significant Upgrade for the Business

Adient has quite a few positives going for it at this time. This includes very solid 2025A results.

The legacy contracts, which have weighed down much of its business and profitability for some years, similar to companies like Alstom (ALSMY), are expected to taper off and go away in the next few years - 2026 to 2027. This is likely to improve company margins step-by-step, so expect these improvements to come in the near term. Some analysts mention the company's JV's/growth foci in China, but given the overall risk profile of both automotive as well as China, I won't be considering this an advantage to Adient. This is not a unique discount I do for Adient - most of the automotives, including legacy ones like BMW and Mercedes, have China "plans" and pushes - I don't give them any consideration for this either and usually discount them as well.

Overall, some of the arguments used to positively describe Adient as an investment, such as having to rethink the seating space or realizing the value in in-house production, are dependent on a world where the customers for Adient actually want to keep things as they are. The flip side is that manufacturers bringing it in-house get cost control, cost reduction, faster turnarounds, and higher supply chain resilience - things that are becoming more and more important in today's world. While many legacy automakers are still in the process of outsourcing, others like Tesla (TSLA) as well as some Chinese ones have started or have always done the seating in-house.

I believe that this, at the very least, puts companies like Adient on watch and their customers in a position where they can negotiate and pressure manufacturers on price and quality. Given that it's an alternative for them to do it themselves, I believe the fundamental upside, or positivity argument, of the seating sector isn't as big as some bullish perspectives want to make it seem.

That doesn't mean it's not attractive at the right price - it's one of the reasons I invest in Lear and Magna (MGA), but it does require significant consideration and discounting. Whenever an analyst tells you that "the market doesn't understand the upside/fundamentals," or some version of that, I've found that usually, it's the analyst or that perspective that's missing some crucial risk or part of the puzzle. I'm not saying (obviously) that irrational undervaluation doesn't happen - that's obviously what I count on. But judging by some targets, Adient has been undervalued for well over 5-6 years. Generally, I tend to view such positive theses with more than a bit of skepticism.

The lack of a yield for the company is problematic - but it's not the only thing. The forecast accuracy (very poor, more than 85% negative forecast accuracy even with a 10-20% margin of error), the market cap (less than £2B), and the credit rating (junk-rated, at a BB-rating) all contribute to my view that the company isn't necessarily all that cheap. Some might argue that by 2027-2028E, the company is expected to almost double its AEPS, with a 25-30% annualized AEPS growth rate.

But with the forecast accuracy that this company has, I would give such estimates very limited, if any, weight - especially when combined with operations in such a volatile sector and market.

Adient is undercovered on Seeking Alpha. The last article was more than 3 months ago - Quant has it at Hold, Wall Street at a Buy average. The problem that I see with the positive ratings is the trends that I mention above. Valuation is technically attractive if the company's earnings and margins improve. But this is something that bulls have said for years. The company, for instance, formed a JV with Boeing (BA) for business-class seating well over 7 years ago - this has still failed to significantly and positively impact the company's AEPS or margins. The thesis of a "turnaround story" has been one that's been parroted for many years now.

Can 2026-2027E be the period where we finally see it materialize?

Maybe - but again, there's a lot of uncertainty in that. Just as before, one of the more difficult arguments for Adient bulls, or anyone positive on this company, to handle is why Adient should be invested in above its peers.

It's fair to say that in a "full bullish" scenario, Adient has a triple-digit upside. I can agree with that. The problem is, sector peers like Lear do as well - and it doesn't even require the company to outperform all that much.

Valuation for Adient - Risks Are Too High When Compared to the Sector

Technically, Adient has a triple-digit upside when looking at 2026-2027E forecasts, both from the company itself and some of the analysts covering it. My argument is that the upside seen above, in companies like Lear, is not only equal or comparable but better because of the inherent safeties of the company.

Lear is A-rated, has a yield (Adient doesn't even have one), and has better forecast accuracy. What's even more, the company doesn't require more than 9-10% AEPS growth here to generate that sort of alpha, and it generates it at a substantially lower, <15x P/E than Adient.

You could make an argument that Adient's total potential upside is higher - but again, this assumes/works with a lot of uncertainties, which, in my view, makes it a lot trickier.

Despite management itself saying that it does not expect much organic growth for 2025-2026E, some analysts have maintained a $60+ share price target for this company trading at less than $25/share. To illustrate the exuberance of this expectation, I can say that this would represent over 25-27x P/E of current AEPS, or 17-18x P/E for 2027-2028E. Possible? Sure - but likely? Not so much, in my opinion.

I expect no more than a 1-2% growth on the top line, and I don't model significant improvements, more along the lines of 50-100 bps of operational improvements, which is well below other companies' margin of 6-8% for the seating segment. The assumption that Adient could match, or should match, Lear's seating segment margin is a logical one, but just because A gets X does not mean that B, which does the same thing, should also get X. That's a too simplified view - yet it's a view that's represented by some analysts covering Adient, justified by succeeding in reaching a 7-8% EBITDA margin (management mentioned this 1.5 years ago).

Overall, I think the upside and assumptions for the company are way too positive to be considered accurate. A market-beating upside is possible. I'd forecast Adient at 13-14x P/E, which incidentally also corresponds to the company's 12-year average, and which implies my specified fair value of $26/share at an AEPS of about $2/share. The assumption that some analysts are giving that it should be a lot higher (Morningstar, for instance, has a fair value estimate of $64/share for the company, while some S&P Global go to $30/share or above) assumes either a more than doubling of AEPS or a premiumization of above 25-27x P/E.

Either way does not work for me.

Because I don't see a realistic turnout for that, and also because I see better alternatives for 2026E, my rating for Adient remains at a Hold. I believe positive assumptions here may be misguided, and in any case, that alternatives on the market are better and more qualitative.

I reiterate a price target, or PT, of $21/share, and even at that point, I'd compare it to sector alternatives before buying. I say this despite a decent/good 2025 performance, with sales up and EBITDA margins up - because no matter the results here, alternatives are still attractive.

Risks to Adient

The positive could, theoretically, materialize. If we see a combination of restructuring upside coupled with massive China volume growth and corresponding earnings growth, we could see earnings 2x the current level, certainly justifying a higher P/E for the company. The company's focus on automation in China could pay off, and if this position in the market is strengthened, further upside is not out of the picture. Adient may, in positive sets of expectations, be worth over $30/share.

However, I view the risk overhang as too great. This risk overhang, as of 2026, I believe these risks include the company's push into Chinese seating with its JV, the cyclical nature of the automotive industry (not exactly a secret, is it?), the fact that it's still in the middle of a significant turnaround effort after so many years, and the very real risk of automotive companies turning to an in-house production model to save money, even at the expense of potentially lower-quality seating. There's also the risk, with the way the market is looking, of overcapacity. Chip shortage is another risk that needs to be considered before investing here.

In short, it's high-level general industry trends that make up the primary sort of risks here - but they are important to consider, especially in this industry. It's too volatile, and the company's share price history shows us that.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Adient is a company that was previously part of Johnson Controls International and represents the world leader in certain types of automotive seating. It's a fundamentally attractive business, but it's one in a bit of a turnaround, and without a yield.

Due to the material undervaluation in the sector, the lack of a dividend yield, and what I consider to be a risky and China-heavy growth prospect, I give the company a fair value of $26/share and a price target of $21/share as of January of 2026. That means that right now, Adient is a Hold, not a Buy.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $21/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Hold rating as of January 2026.

data.title: YOLO: Exposure To Cannabis Stocks Leans Into Politics
data.description:
The AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF (YOLO) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to companies that operate in the cannabis industry on a global basis. YOLO’s performance may be influenced by a number of factors, including changes in public policy, company cash flows, and M&A, amongst a number of other factors. Given the nature of the companies held within the strategy, YOLO may not be an appropriate investment vehicle for all investors.

About AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF

YOLO was launched by AdvisorShares on April 17, 2019 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. YOLO is an actively managed investment strategy, meaning that the performance of the fund is influenced by the expertise of the portfolio management team in security selection and weighting. YOLO has a net expense ratio of 51bps, a relatively low fee for an actively managed investment strategy.

At the time of writing, YOLO is a narrowly traded ETF with $40 million in net assets and an average of $426 thousand in share value traded on a daily basis. This compares to AdvisorShares’ sister strategy (MSOS), which has $885 million in net assets and $58 million in daily trading volumes. Despite the relatively low liquidity in YOLO, the ETF offers investors with similar exposure with a relatively lower fee; MSOS has a net expense ratio of 78bps, though offers substantially greater liquidity.

There are two critical differences between YOLO and MSOS:

  1. Securities used for investment.
  2. Geographical exposure.

YOLO was designed to provide investors with global exposure whereas MSOS will only provide investors with US-domiciled companies. Accordingly, US exposure makes up roughly 69% of net assets; Canada 29%, the UK 1.8%, and Israel 0.57% [note rounding is used].

YOLO directly invests in the shares of the individual holdings whereas MSOS utilizes swap derivatives to gain exposure. That being said, currently 46% of net assets in YOLO are allocated to MSOS. Though there is some overlap in terms of positioning in companies like Village Farms International, Inc. (VFF) and FLUENT Corp. (CNTMF), overlap between the two strategies net of the direct investment in MSOS by YOLO is minimal. Given the significant allocation in MSOS, I believe investors should understand the mechanics of the fund as the performance of YOLO may be heavily influenced by the performance of MSOS. You can review my coverage of MSOS here.

The current top equity holdings within YOLO include Village Farms International at 19%, High Tide Inc. (HITI) at 9%, and SNDL Inc. (SNDL) at 4.27%. In total, YOLO is made up of 18 individual equity positions along with its position in MSOS. YOLO is largely made up of small-cap and micro-cap equities, making up nearly 97% of net assets.

From an investment perspective, I believe cannabis stocks may be most heavily influenced by public policy relating to industry- and drug-related regulation. In December 2025, President Trump signed an executive order to loosen restrictions on cannabis, shifting the substance from Schedule 1 to Schedule 3. The executive order eased regulations for banking, tax restrictions, and research, a positive policy shift for the industry. A major benefit that came out of the executive order was the Medicare pilot program that provides CBD therapies into federally insured health care programs for seniors, potentially broadening the marketability for CBD products.

Though 30 states have decriminalized marijuana for recreational and medical use with others providing mixed regulations for medical CBD and THC, a federal shift in policy may create a more open environment for the substance that may positively impact the broader industry. In terms of value drivers for YOLO, further state and federal deregulation may positively influence the portfolio’s performance. Conversely, the opposite would be true if regulatory conditions became more restrictive for this industry.

Investor Suitability

YOLO can be used as both a buy-and-hold investment vehicle as well as part of an actively managed trading strategy given the event-driven risk involved in the portfolio companies. Given that YOLO and MSOS are actively managed, investors seeking to gain exposure to the cannabis industry can hold these ETFs as a long-term investment strategy for capital appreciation. Given the volatile nature of the industry, these ETFs can also be used for short-term tactical positioning. Given that these funds do not currently provide a distribution, investors should only utilize them for expected capital returns.

Because of the nature of fund with this focus, advisors should consider political, religious, and ethical views of their clients prior to making a final investment decision.

Risks Related to YOLO

Companies held in YOLO operate in a gray area of the market where legality of the substance may be of concern, potentially making the strategy less appropriate for some. The performance of YOLO may be influenced by political risk as changes to public policy may have a greater impact on performance relative to company fundamentals.

YOLO exhibits relatively thin liquidity and bears an average bid/ask spread of 1.37%, potentially impacting the cost-in/cost-out of an investor’s trading strategy.

Final Thoughts

YOLO is an actively managed investment strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the cannabis industry. Investors may weigh political and policy risk as part of their investment and trading strategy when considering an investment in YOLO. Prior to investing in YOLO, I believe investors should be aware of the mechanics involved in the performance of MSOS given the significant investment concentration in the portfolio.

This article answers three questions about YOLO:

  1. What kind of stocks are in YOLO's portfolio?
  2. What influences YOLO's performance?
  3. Which investors is YOLO suitable for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Intel: Wildly Bipolar Outcome
data.description:
We previously discussed Intel Corporation (INTC) and its big coming earnings day earlier this month. This was, of course, a rare public Strong Buy rating for the public site in April of 2025, and we moved to hold at is it got to about $50+ encouraging some profit taking just on the turnaround being largely price in. Now the company must execute, as the Street has bid the stock up. Well, the big day has arrived and the company just-reported earnings. Here we discuss results relative to our previously laid out expectations.

Our Expectations For Intel's Q4 2025

So what did we expect for Q4 earnings? We were anticipating results that were down from a year ago, and this was due to operational and strategic decisions with company business lines that we knew would impact comps. Moving forward for the entire year 2026, we expect growth from 2025, though expected that inflection really to start heavily in the spring and summer of the year.

Intel's Q4 2025 Earnings Revenue And Margin Expectations

For Q4, our expectations were to see some declines from last year, but to also get a guidance that showed performance stabilization in 2026. We discussed previously that we would be focused heavily on margins. Margins have been on the mend. With cost cuts and other efficiencies we thought we would see margins that hit 37% as adjusted. We were looking for revenue to come in at the higher end of expectations and were targeting $13.3-$13.9 billion, significantly higher than the midpoint of management's guide (which is $13.3 billion).

Intel's Q4 2025 Earnings EPS And Cash Flow

On the EPS front, we expected a swing back to positive territory. We expected earnings of $0.09-$0.12 per share in the quarter, which was also above the guide, though dipping from last year. Client computing we thought would be up mid-single digits in the report, largely from restructuring of the old Network and Edge segment. Overall, we expected product revenue to come in flat to up low to mid-single digits. We also expected ongoing debt reduction and deleveraging in the Q4 report and continue to expect this in 2026. We anticipated operating cash flow of $2.2-$2.5 billion and free cash flow $200-$400 million, depending on product mix and final capital expenditures. So how did we do?

Intel's Q4 2025 Earnings Performance

So, in Q4, the company delivered a surprise beat and revenues that were down 4% from the year prior as we expected. We were correct that revenues would be significantly higher than expected We have to highlight the margin outcome. It was a 210 basis point decline in adjusted gross margin to 37.9%, compared to 40% in the sequential Q3, and down from 42.1% from a year ago but better than guided and better than we expected. There were declines in R&D spending, worth noting, but operating margin was down 80 basis points to 8.8% from last year but also better than expected. The EPS line came in at $0.15 which was $0.04 better than our expectations, and again, our expectations were more bullish than most.

Now turning to segment performance, we would like to remind you that in Q1 2025, Network and Edge was integrated into client computing and the data center/AI segments. Impacting comps as well as the fact that Altera was deconsolidated in Q3. That said, in Q4, client computing saw revenue fall 7% from last year to $8.2 billion. We thought it would be a little better. Further, data centers and AI were up 9% from last year to $4.7 billion, better than we expected. And as far as Intel Foundry, it reported revenues that were up 4% to $4.5 billion. Make no mistake, it is undeniable that a lot of transitions have occurred in the last year. In fact, we still think referring to it as the “New Intel” is not necessarily incorrect, but we call in the more 'focused Intel."

Finally, our cash flow expectations were surpassed mightily. Well, in Q4 operating cash flow strong, and we saw gross capital expenditures of about $4 billion in the quarter and positive adjusted free cash flow of $2.2 billion, which was substantially better than we expected. To us, that was the biggest positive surprise of the quarter. With a cash influx and positive free cash flow, the company paid down $3.75 billion in debt in Q4. We expect further debt reduction in 2026.

Forward View On Intel Corporation And INTC Stock

This was a solid quarter overall. We were correct in being more bullish than most on the results. However, it is not always about where a company has been, but where it is going. So, what about looking ahead? We know that, coming into this report, the company was widely expected to grow EPS 70-80% each of the next 3 years, which is why shares were bid back up.

For Q1, the company expects revenue of $11.7 to $12.7 billion. This is actually below consensus at the midpoint, and could be a reason shares may fall. Hard to say as we craft this immediately after release, but that guide comes following a solid Q4 2025. Adjusted gross margin guidance of 34.5% seems light as well, though, for EPS, it is forecasted breakeven adjusted. That is also 5 cents below consensus. So, this is a disappointing guide, all things considered.

That said, we think 2026, still sees a nice inflection, but INTC shares were priced for a better outlook overall. We think shares remain at Hold, and/or some profit taking (shave some of the position) if you doubled or more is warranted here at $53.

data.title: XAR And SHLD: Chips And Security, Greenland Crisis The End Of NATO?
data.description:
Following the news that European countries are sending military units to Greenland to explore opportunities for a NATO mission on Greenland, President Trump has slammed the countries with a 10% tariff only to revert them later following a conversation with NATO leader Mark Rutte. While not immediate, the developments regarding Greenland are concerning for the stability of NATO as well as global stability. In this report, I discuss the importance of Greenland, the weakness in President Trump’s argument to take ownership of Greenland, and how that may affect U.S. and European aerospace and defense equities.

Is Greenland Really of Security Importance?

The fact that President Trump pursues ownership of Greenland is not new. President Trump already floated the idea to buy Greenland for security purposes in 2019. I believe there is a defensible case for Greenland being of security interest to the United States in the Arctic region.

Greenland is strategically positioned to monitor Russian submarine movements towards North America as well as airplane movements. The shortest path from Russia to the United States passes over the Arctic region. As a result, Greenland is strategically positioned as a linchpin for missile and early-warning defense, such as the to-be-developed Golden Dome.

Justification to Take Control of Greenland is Dangerous

One of several arguments that President Trump makes is that Denmark and European countries cannot defend Greenland from threats such as Russia and China. While that may be true, the United States taking control of Greenland is barely a solution for national and global security. As an alliance, NATO could step up defense presence to safeguard the interests of all NATO partners in the region. The President quite often portrays Europe as incapable of defending itself or Greenland. Interestingly, as I discussed in a publication in October 2025, Europe has been successfully defending itself against Russian drone incursions. As an example, we also note that while the U.S. Navy dominates warfare, countries such as the Netherlands are punching far above their weight. You could speak about subsystem leadership, where the Dutch Navy excels in radar technology through the SMART-L MM/N radar, able to provide ballistic missile detection and multi-target engagement through the APAR or Active Phased Array Radar with an overall superior sensor fusion philosophy.

So, taking control of Greenland is not a solution for national security interests.

National security interests also do not provide a justification to take control of Greenland. By the same logic, the following shifts could occur:

  • Canada takes Greenland and Alaska.
  • The United States, Mexico, and several countries in South America could claim French and Dutch overseas areas in the Caribbean.
  • Russia could take Ukraine to build a buffer between European countries and Moscow.
  • China can take Taiwan.

President Trump also implied that just because Danish ships landed on the coasts of Greenland does not mean they have any right to Greenland. That is problematic for several reasons. The first one is that this approach towards questioning the sovereignty of a country or area is the very same approach that Russia used in Ukraine. If all countries were to do that, we would have conflict zones arising in many continents today rather than tomorrow.

Secondly, the United States taking Greenland from a fellow NATO member is not a good look for the unity of NATO, which has already been dwindling, and only empowers countries like China and Russia. It is often forgotten that following the 9/11 attacks and the wars that followed in Iraq and Afghanistan, NATO allies stood with the United States. NORAD scrambled U.S. and Canadian fighter jets to patrol the U.S. skies, and following the activation of Article 5, NATO aircraft and crews flew hundreds of missions providing radar surveillance and airborne early warning in the North American airspace.

The NORAD mission is called Operation Noble Eagle and is actually still ongoing, and the NATO mission was called Operation Eagle Assist, lasting from October 2001 through 2002, and ended when the United States upgraded air defense and improved cooperation between civil and military authorities in the United States. What we quite often see is that these missions showing how the United States relied on allies and NATO have been removed from the collective memory. Going against a fellow NATO country immediately removes the unity that the US one day could count on when in need, making China and Russia a bigger threat than they already are today.

So, taking Greenland could lead to a global eruption of conflict zones, strengthen the position of the real adversaries, and be an incredibly high risk for the modern world that relies on chips, as it means that the United States and West no longer have any leverage whatsoever to convince China not to take Taiwan, which is the world’s center of chip manufacturing.

Can the United States Leave NATO?

I would say that while NATO still stands today and Greenland-related tariffs have been cancelled, the unity between European countries and the US is diminishing. President Trump has threatened to leave NATO several times, but that is easier said than done. Article 13 of the NATO treaty allows for a country to leave NATO one year after announcing withdrawal. Furthermore, under section 1250A “Limitation on withdrawal from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization” of the 2024 National Defense Authorization Act, congressional approval is required to withdraw from NATO through a two-thirds majority vote or an Act of Congress. The President can veto a decision from Congress, but that veto could be countered by a two-thirds majority in the House and the Senate. While NATO has been regarded as the United States cradling European allies, generally there is support for NATO, so leaving NATO is not easy, and we also note that the United States typically has around 80,000-100,000 U.S. troops on European soil, and those do not only satisfy the NATO security interests but also the U.S. interests for power projection and keeping allies aligned. So, the United States has a vested interest that goes beyond the security capabilities that NATO provides.

Strategic Trade Land and Resources Play a Bigger Role

So, Greenland is of interest from a security perspective. However, the bigger driver of why the United States would be interested in Greenland centers on strategic positioning and strategic resources. That is very much in the same way minerals became a focal point in the Ukraine-U.S.-Russia framework.

The following strategic elements are of interest and increasingly becoming accessible due to climate change:

  • Energy Resources
    • Oil & Gas
    • Uranium
  • Critical minerals and rare earths
    • Rare Earth Elements
    • Lithium
    • Graphite
    • Nickel
    • Cobalt
    • Copper
    • Zinc
    • Uranium
  • Shipping routes
    • Northern Sea Route, which cuts Asia-Europe transit by 30-40 percent.
    • Northwest Passage as alternative to Panama/Suez.
  • Food security
    • Fishery
    • Fresh water; The Arctic holds 20% of the world’s fresh water.

What we are generally seeing is that in the bigger picture, the national security interests are present, but the bigger reason why the United States would be interested in Greenland focuses on strategic resources, trade control, and food security. The United States has been engaged in a chip war with China, and the reality is that from a resource perspective, the United States is set to lose that chip war every day, and that is why it is important for the United States to press for export controls. The resources in Greenland are critical to semiconductor manufacturing, and effective control over Greenland provides the US access to those critical resources. Other key areas that would benefit from mineral and strategic resource control in Greenland are Aerospace and Defense, Battery and Energy Storage, AI Data Centers, and Power Infrastructure. These are all key areas in which the world is making big steps today. In some way, Greenland hosts the critical minerals that form the foundation of modern civilization’s industrial stack.

Why Push for Greenland Now?

There are several reasons why President Trump makes the push for Greenland now. From a semiconductor perspective, the United States is strategically invested in Intel (INTC), and TSMC (TSM) has an expansion path in Arizona. Access to strategic resources accelerates the path towards becoming a semiconductor and AI force. In some way, it is about bringing silicon back to the US after decades of outsourcing, which led to a high concentration in Taiwan and a dependence on regional stability.

In terms of enabling nature, Greenland can be the U.S. version of Taiwan. That is the entire template that is being followed now. That transformation is not going to happen overnight but fits with the aim of improving domestic capabilities. While the United States is seen as an advanced economy, the reality is that for advanced lithography, the country depends on Dutch EUV machines, and Intel missed the EUV train and is currently trying to catch up, while TSMC’s presence in the US and other parts of the world is strategically motivated. The only sense that the US is winning the chip race comes from export controls.

From a defense perspective, President Trump has the military momentum. The intervention in Venezuela put U.S. military power on display, and we also saw that the world generally watched the United States intervention in a sovereign country. That is not to say that the transition of power is not a good one for Venezuela, but borders and sovereignty are being blurred.

We also note that access to strategic resources has been a pain point for some aerospace and defense companies. One example is DRS, which faced some challenges in sourcing Germanium after China imposed a now-halted ban on dual-use materials. The painful reality for the U.S. military-industrial complex is that it is used as a deterrent force against the likes of Russia, China, and lately also Europe, but over 78% of the weapon systems are dependent on China-sourced minerals. So, the actual power projection is less strong than it seems.

Furthermore, using data from the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute, I have projected the defense budget path for Canada, the United States, and Europe. What we see is that, if nothing changes, by 2028 the United States will no longer be the dominant force measured by dollar value in the current budget path. By 2029, Europe will account for 50% of the defense budget and more than half by the end of the decade. In the White House they are likely also aware of this, and that is one of the reasons why President Trump is floating the idea of a $1.5 trillion defense budget and why pressure is being applied fast. From a budget perspective, the United States stands to lose leverage in the years ahead on the current budget trajectory.

We have seen President Trump push for higher defense spending for other NATO members for years, and while that may have been a good move if you view things as if the United States is providing some sort of welfare security service, the consequence is that an expansion of budgets in European countries will ultimately lead to a new military power block, which could shift global power relations, and that is something that President Trump had not kept in mind. It is likely also the reason why previous administrations have not aggressively sought an acceleration in defense spending; it was simply beneficial to have a lower-budget partner to control rather than forcing higher budgets and losing leverage in the process. By pushing defense spending higher for EU countries, President Trump has aligned the United States to lose some of its power projection capability, which also plays a big role in geopolitics and trade.

What Does This All Mean for the U.S. Aerospace and Defense Industry?

Generally, the push for higher defense budgets is a positive for U.S. Aerospace and Defense companies. However, with the increasing rhetoric from President Trump, we are seeing European countries increasingly being pushed away from relying on the United States. I already discussed this increase in self-reliance in a report published last year, and it also led to European defense ETFs outperforming U.S. peers for a while, although I do not believe it paves the way for structural outperformance.

I have assessed over $400 billion worth of sales for holdings in the XAR ETF, which is a quarterly rebalancing U.S. Defense ETF with a $0.35% expense ratio and a $4.7 billion AUM with holdings as shown below:

What I found is that around 70% of the sales are attributed to the United States, 10% to Europe, and 19% to other countries, some of which could include European sales, as not all companies provide the same level of granularity in geographic sales. So, $40 billion in sales exposure to Europe at least, plus contracts through the Foreign Military Sales Program. I would also not say that these sales are directly at risk. That is because even with Europe’s push to become more self-sufficient for defense needs, it constitutes a multi-year push in which the European defense industrial complex has to speed up, and reliance on U.S. products continues.

What Are the Prospects for European Defense Companies?

At the same time, I believe that the latest friction between European countries and the United States is going to push Europe even more to rebuild the European defense industry, which obviously benefits European defense stocks. However, there is a limit to that. The industry currently is not yet sized to absorb all demand from European countries, nor are the capabilities developed. We also see that there is no strong mechanism in the renewed defense focus demanding Euros in the budgets to be spent with European contractors. Europe commands 65% of the content of systems to be sourced in the EU or an associated country. That seems like a blow to U.S. defense contractors, but it is not. This rule only holds for the European Defence Industry Program, which has €1.5 billion in funding for 2025-2027. So, it is largely symbolic, as the EU likely is aware that the industry does not have the capacity or required capabilities yet to be fully independent for security needs. At the same time, part of the strategy likely also involves attracting joint ventures with U.S. companies to accelerate the development of the European industry. For that reason, I am not only bullish on the prospects of XAR, but also SHLD, which has exposure to European as well as U.S. defense stocks:

SHLD, which has a $6.9 billion AUM, a 0.5% expense ratio, and a dividend yield of 0.5% with semi-annual payments and market cap weighting, in some sense presents a mix of the stability in the U.S. defense stocks and potential high-growth opportunities for European defense stocks.

If we look at the performance between XAR, which is pure U.S. aerospace exposure; SHLD, which is blended; and WisdomTree European Defense Fund (WDEF), which is a pure play on European defense stocks, we see that all ETFs outperformed the market year-to-date with no major exposure-driven benefit for one ETF or the other.

Over a one-year period, we even note that the EU-focused ETF underperformed relative to SHLD and XAR. So, XAR and SHLD, in my view, are the better picks. What I like about XAR is that it also includes some commercial exposure through companies such as Boeing (BA), Hexcel (HXL), and HEICO (HEI.A), ATI (ATI), Howmet Aerospace (HWM), and General Dynamics (GD) on top of the pure-defense names, while SHLD includes names with potential high growth in the defense business.

Conclusion: NATO Turmoil Helps European Defense Companies

I believe that the turmoil triggered by the wishes to annex Greenland provides some support for higher stock prices for EU-based defense contractors. However, that comes with the big sidenote that for the moment, such an increase in the stock price would mostly be sentiment-driven as it does not substantially add to near-term opportunities. It just justifies a higher trading multiple as the realization is increasing that European countries need to continue striving for sovereign security solutions with a strong European defense complex. So, the Greenland crisis does not mark the end of NATO, but it does serve as a validation that Europe needs its own defense solutions, and if we look at the current budget developments, we see European rising as a defense power measured by dollars in the budget and that could eventually be a catalyst for U.S. defense equities to rally as it provides credible support for the United States to increase its defense budgets as well and maintain power as a military power to drive its geopolitical and trade agenda. It is either that, or the United States has to start viewing Europe as a partner.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: This Party Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Stating that Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) was a steal at all-time highs back in early October was probably one of my best calls of the year. In that analysis, I found explosive growth in their cloud memory segment and margin expansion. Meanwhile, the valuation was still attractive. Since then, Micron has surged an astonishing 108% as of this writing to set all-time high after all-time high. In today's article, I will be reviewing their latest earnings and will also be discussing key recent developments and where the valuation is now.

Below, it is shown that Micron's Q1 was an extraordinary quarter. Growth is picking up, and margin performance was simply jaw-dropping. While their results were impressive, Q2 guidance was perhaps even more encouraging. Top-line growth is expected to speed up, and margin expansion is set to accelerate. While there are some risks of an AI bubble forming, I would say that with the forward P/E at such a low level, there is a good margin for error. As a result, I'm reiterating my strong buy rating for Micron stock.

Financial Performance

Let's start off with an overview of the company's most recent financial results. Micron was able to generate total revenues of $13.6 billion for its FY2026 Q1. As you can see above, an inflection point for top-line growth was hit a while ago, and now Q1 represents their second straight quarter of significant acceleration. With the revenue growth rate at 57%, I would say that it's pretty clear that Micron's business is booming. Later, we'll discuss supply and see how it may be affecting the company's growth right now.

In Q1, Micron destroyed expectations as they beat them by a margin of $760.74 million. Therefore, this really was a monstrous quarter in terms of outperformance. Let's turn to their revenues by technology.

As shown above, both DRAM and NAND saw robust levels of growth. That said, it really was DRAM that took most of the spotlight, as 79% of total revenues were from this technology. With growth being 69% for DRAM in the quarter, there is no question that this is a leading driver for the company moving forward. Nonetheless, the results in NAND were no slouch and show that there is broad strength in Micron's results.

Margin Expansion Is Breathtaking

In the vast majority of Micron's business, margin expansion was simply breathtaking. In their Core Data unit, they saw just 1 percentage point of gross margin expansion YoY and actually saw some operating margin contraction. However, that is unable to offset the bullishness of the other units' margin excellence.

As you can see, margins in Cloud Memory, Mobile and Client, and Automotive and Embedded saw expansion that would be better described by percentage points rather than basis points. We'll discuss the memory shortage later, but it's pretty obvious that the pricing power of Micron has increased significantly in recent times. For the company as a whole, they saw gross margin expand by 17.3 percentage points while operating margin improved by 19.5 percentage points. These numbers are honestly off the charts.

In terms of the bottom line, things don't get less impressive. For Q1, Micron reported an adjusted EPS of $4.78, representing a growth rate of 167% YoY. This is miles above the top-line growth rate, and that reflects the breathtaking margins discussed above. In addition, they were able to fly past bottom-line expectations as well as beat them by $0.82. Moving forward, you can see that analyst consensus calls for further growth acceleration before some moderation. Do note, however, that while growth may seem low for FY2027 Q1, it is still expected to be at a whopping 126.11%.

Supply Shortages

By now, it is no secret that AI has created memory shortages, and for Micron, that's, of course, a sign of unrelenting demand. As Chris Ciaccia of Seeking Alpha writes,

"memory, in particular, high-bandwidth memory, has experienced a shortage as data centers and GPUs gobble up memory for their needs."

As a sign that this is a massive boost for margins, it was reported earlier this month that Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK hynix (HXSC.F) were considering raising server memory prices by a staggering 70% in Q1. For Micron, that is clearly an indication that the margin strength discussed above will continue at least for the near term while supply shortages remain.

In the longer run, there are good indications that the outlook is bright. The company now forecasts that their TAM will hit $100 billion in 2028, and that represents around a 40% CAGR. Next, let's discuss how they aim to capture the opportunity at hand.

Fabrication Push

On Jan 16th, it was reported that Micron broke ground on their $100 billion memory manufacturing site in New York state. While plans for this fab started a while ago, this manufacturing push should help to alleviate supply concerns in the long run. This facility is likely to become operational in 2030, and so the company has to find a quicker solution to address shortages. That's where Taiwan comes into play.

Just a day after the groundbreaking for the New York fab, it was reported that Micron is planning to buy a fab site in Taiwan from Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation for $1.8 billion. As of now, this site is on track to provide a significant amount of DRAM wafers in CY2027 H2, and so this is expected to help with the shortage much faster than the New York fab.

At this point, it seems that Micron will continue to benefit from immense pricing power in the near term, while for the long term, they are pushing into fabrication in an attempt to secure their market share. I believe this is a winning strategy.

Q2 Outlook

Let's take a look at their Q2 financial outlook. At the $18.7 billion midpoint for revenue, Micron is implying top-line growth of 132% YoY. That is steep acceleration from Q1's 57% and is a reflection of the trends that we have discussed in today's article. For gross margin, they are expecting 68% at the midpoint. That would be a 30.1 percentage point expansion YoY. I thought Q1's YoY improvement was impressive, but this is just extraordinary profitability. For operating expenses, they are projecting $1.38 billion at the midpoint, up 32% YoY. Considering the expected top-line growth rate, this increase actually shows that corporate efficiency is set to improve. Lastly, for the bottom line, the midpoint EPS estimate of $8.42 represents a 440% increase YoY. I believe no comment is necessary for that kind of growth.

Valuation Is Unbelievably Cheap

In my previous article, Micron's forward P/E was at 11.22. Since then, there has been very modest expansion to 11.78 despite the stock's large gains, and so I believe Micron remains a bargain now.

In today's analysis, I found truly remarkable fundamentals. Their Q1 results were nearly flawless, and both the near- and longer-term outlooks have been shown to be bright as the demand for memory is relentless. With Micron benefitting from huge margins right now and planning a fabrication push to secure the long-term future of the company, I would say that they are very well positioned.

While it's obvious that EPS upward revisions have weighed on the forward P/E, I believe current levels show that there is plenty of room for multiple expansion moving forward. Note that Seeking Alpha shows Micron's non-GAAP forward P/E is at a nearly 53% discount to the IT sector as of this writing. In my view, this is enough to show that the risk/reward remains deeply in the bulls' favor.

Risks

It can be inferred that the TAM forecasts are based on the assumption that the AI boom will continue strongly over the next few years. The company's fabrication push likely also has this assumption. While this may be a fair assumption to have based on what we're seeing right now, there is some risk that a bubble is forming in AI. Just today (Jan 22nd), OpenAI (OPENAI) Chairman Bret Taylor expressed that there is “probably” a bubble. Specifically, he stated that

I think over the next few years, you’ll see a correction, you also see consolidation, but I don’t think you can get innovation without that kind of messy competition.

Therefore, while AI will ultimately become a revolutionary technology much like the internet, there will be bumps along the road. This may cause short-term dips in CAPEX confidence, and AI hardware providers like Micron could see some headwinds vis-a-vis their financial performance. Since the forward P/E discussed above is based on strong growth projections, if spending pulls back, there is a risk of stock underperformance.

That said, I do believe the incredibly low level in the multiple provides a good margin for error, and so I remain very bullish despite some risks of a bubble forming.

Takeaway

Where should I start? Back in October when I upgraded Micron to a strong buy, I envisioned strong returns and a booming business. However, the amount of momentum they are seeing now is truly beyond my expectations by a significant amount. The great thing is that while the fundamentals and outlook have improved immensely, the forward P/E ratio is little changed, and so I believe the valuation still offers an ample amount of opportunity to investors. As discussed above, there are some risks of an AI bubble forming in the near term, but I would say that the valuation is low enough to offer some comfort. Furthermore, in the long run, AI will undoubtedly be a revolutionary technology, and so even if there is near-term turbulence, Micron ultimately stands to benefit. With that in mind, I believe a reiteration of my strong buy rating is justified.

data.title: Reinsurance Group Of America: Lock In 2% 'Alpha' With The Baby Bonds
data.description:
The overall market isn't signaling any problems, but we are really scared. There has been just too much news lately. It seems that investors are more worried about the US's credibility than about equity markets. Long-term bonds have a hard time catching a rally. On the contrary, corporate debt is still priced at historically low credit spreads, and we are constantly looking for opportunities to earn extra returns without taking on too much credit risk. This is the case with Reinsurance Group of America (RGA) and its baby bonds.

Reinsurance Group of America Brief Information

RGA was founded in 1973 and is headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. The company is one of the largest life insurance reinsurers in the world and operates in over 25 countries. Its total assets for the third quarter of 2025 were close to $115 billion, and its current market capitalization is around $12.78 billion.

In the snapshot below, you can see how rating agencies evaluate RGA and its subsidiaries:

And RGA's credit ratings:

The chart shows how highly correlated RGA is with the State Street SPDR S&P Insurance ETF (KIE):

With companies like RGA, we are posting just a short review of the company so that the reader can get familiar with how safe its debt is. The sweet spot of the article is to follow as we explain where the alpha is hidden.

Baby Bonds

RGA has two baby bonds (RZB) and (RZC):

Both bonds are currently rated with an "investment-grade" credit rating: S&P rates with "BBB+", Moody's with "Baa2", and Fitch with "BBB-". RZC has a yield to worst of close to 6.31% (at $25.42).

RZC is a high-duration baby bond with a maturity date of approximately 27 years, set for 10/15/2052. The bond is reset-rate security. Its call date is 10/15/2027, with about two years to call. The bond was issued on 09/15/2022 with a coupon rate of 7.13% and a spread to the 20-year Treasury yield of approximately 3.13%. After its call date, this rate will be equal to the sum of the 5-year U.S. Treasury yield on the applicable fixed rate calculation date plus 3.456%, resetting every five years thereafter on the applicable fixed rate calculation date.

Based on market consensus:

RZC is about to reset at a nominal yield higher than 7%. If we use a 5-year yield of 4.09%, the reset rate is expected to be close to 7.55%. This high expected nominal yield, combined with the company's credibility, makes RZC a very probable redemption. In fact, if the security is not redeemed on its call date, it will most likely trade as a pinned-to-par security, gravitating around par. An investor in RZC is expected to lock in the 6.30% yield to call in less than two years. To explain how generous this yield is, we have to look at the senior bonds of the company that are not listed on the primary exchanges.

OTC Bonds

Above are shown RGA's over-the-counter bonds, rated "A1" and "Baa1". These bonds are senior to RZC and, as a general rule, are expected to trade with lower yields. We view the redemption probability of RZC as very high, given its high expected nominal yield on its reset date. The company's short-duration senior bonds yield close to 4%. The YTC of RZC comes with a spread of more than 2% for going two notches lower in credit quality within the same company. We have seen this phenomenon across many fixed-to-floating and reset-rate securities. The market seems to leave some Alpha on the table by failing to assess the risk-reward opportunity properly. For such a highly rated company as RGA, this spread has to be lower than 0.5% for such a low period of time.

Fair Value of RZC

We will set a 5% yield to call as the bond's fair value. This represents a spread of more than 0.7% to the senior notes and 1.4% above the 2-year Treasury. To reach 5% YTC, the price of RZC will have to appreciate close to 2%. Such a capital appreciation may not seem particularly spectacular, but we find the risk-adjusted Alpha to be superior to almost any exchange-traded fixed-income instrument at the moment.

Conclusion

The capital structures of well-established investment-grade issuers rarely provide "Alpha" to investors, as they are usually priced by large institutions. We have been able to capitalize on small mispricings in such exchange-traded products, and RZC appears mispriced relative to its peers. We rate it as a buy as long as its yield to worst is higher than 5% and as a sell if it reaches the yield to worst of its higher-rated brothers at around 4.2% yield to call, all else being equal.

data.title: SVOL: Some Uncertainty On The Horizon
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

When we want to monetize short-term market volatility as investors, one of the alternatives we have is to do so in an indexed manner through the Simplify Volatility Premium ETF (SVOL). By doing so, we expose ourselves to daily (short-term) results, which correspond to an exposure of between -0.2 and -0.3 (inverse) of the return on the short-term volatility futures index linked to the VIX. To achieve this exposure, the ETF trades short futures and financial options (put and call) on the VIX, seeking to exploit the carry of volatility as measured by the VIX itself. In other words, it makes money if the prices of the futures it trades fall.

To do this, it is very important to understand the position of the futures curve. This can be in contango (a normal situation, where current futures are worth less than long-term futures) or backwardation (a curve inversion, where short-term futures are more expensive than long-term futures). As these are short-term futures, the fund rolls its position to maintain its desired exposure to the volatility market. When this rolling occurs and the curve is in contango, the ETF makes money, as the market is paying it a “premium” for selling protection, and if nothing serious happens.

If, on the other hand, a specific market shock occurs and the futures curve shifts from contango to backwardation, the ETF will lose money because it will not be able to capture the volatility premium due to the shape of the futures curve. In these cases, futures prices rise sharply, which has a negative impact on a short position. The fund would sell volatility at a high price today and buy it tomorrow at a lower price, so with the rolling of the trade, it would end up losing money. That is why the shape of the VIX futures curve is so important. For these situations, the ETF management team has a call option overlay, which protects the ETF when futures prices rise sharply.

As we will see later, the VIX is currently showing some signs of backwardation at the short end of the curve. As we have seen, this is detrimental to both the price and the carry of the fund. I believe there is too much uncertainty in the short to medium term to recommend holding.
